Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a structured study routine. I'd mostly just wing it whenever I felt like I needed to cram or when something was due soon. I learn best by doing things with my hands or actually practicing, so I'd often wait until I could sit down and work through problems physically rather than just reading. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 20-something hours, but it was scattered and unplanned.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I'm not great with consistency—I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly pull long sessions when I realized something was due. It wasn't intentional; I just found it hard to care enough to stick to a schedule when nothing immediate was pressing me.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or realizing I had no idea what was happening in a course would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, scroll around for a bit, then get bored and leave. The LMS wasn't exactly thrilling, so I needed an actual reason to be there beyond just browsing.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural routine for me. There were periods where I'd check in more regularly because I was watching lecture videos in chunks, but that was more about getting through content than habit. Once I finished a video series, I'd disappear for a while until something else demanded my attention.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful because I could watch them while doing other things or pause and rewind when I needed to actually understand something. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything I could interact with or apply practically stuck better. The videos at least let me control my pace, which helped when I was feeling bored or distracted.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—posting twice the whole course tells you everything. They felt like busywork, and I couldn't bring myself to care about typing out responses that felt performative. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because they seemed redundant if I already got the concept from the video.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it involved actually doing something rather than passively absorbing information. If I could see the practical application or knew it would show up on an exam, I'd engage. Otherwise, it just felt like filler content designed to keep us busy.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If something wasn't required, my brain immediately categorized it as unnecessary, even if it might have been helpful. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them. The optional stuff just couldn't compete with my general sense of boredom and disengagement.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS at all. I also used my own notes heavily—writing things out by hand helps me remember better than clicking through screens. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or other resources that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me.
Q10: There were entire study sessions where I'd be working through practice problems on paper or reviewing printed notes, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes study with a friend in person, talking through concepts and quizzing each other, which was actually really effective but completely invisible online.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, download any associated materials, and then never open that video again in the LMS. My actual studying happened with those downloaded files spread across my desk while I worked through things hands-on. The LMS was just a content delivery system, not where real learning happened for me.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. When the content felt repetitive or when I was just generally bored with life, logging in felt like a chore. My anxiety would spike sometimes too, making it hard to start anything because I'd worry about how much I'd fallen behind. Those periods were rough.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—when I was tired, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Competing responsibilities would also pull me away, and since I wasn't super invested in the course emotionally, it was easy to deprioritize. Confusion sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I was on certain topics.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but I'd be completely zoned out thinking about something else. I'd scroll through pages without actually reading anything, just going through the motions. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean my brain was actually engaged with the material.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. Part of it was boredom—the work just didn't excite me enough to start early. Part of it was my low resilience; when things felt hard, I'd avoid them rather than push through. I knew it was a bad habit, but knowing didn't really change my behavior.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. My LMS activity would spike dramatically right before something was due. Without that external pressure, I'd drift along doing the bare minimum. It's not a great system, but the urgency of a deadline was the only reliable motivator I had.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, especially when I did well—it reminded me I could actually succeed if I tried. But the impact was short-lived. I'd feel good about a high score for a day, then slip back into my usual disengaged patterns. Negative feedback sometimes made me avoid the course even more because it felt discouraging.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which honestly wasn't great for someone like me who needs external pressure. Having so many online courses meant I could always push things off. If there had been more mandatory check-ins or structured milestones, I probably would have stayed more consistent throughout.
Q19: The flexibility made engagement harder because it let me procrastinate without immediate consequences. On the other hand, having video content I could watch at my own pace was helpful for my learning style. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much value, which made me resent participating in them.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to figure out what's important, or maybe just feeling anxious about falling behind without actually being able to produce work. Sometimes you're in the system a lot because you're confused and searching for clarity, not because you're being productive. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's basically me. I performed well on exams because I'm actually good at understanding material when I focus, but my LMS presence was minimal because I studied differently. I'd cram effectively, use offline resources, and show up for what mattered. The system couldn't see my actual learning process.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people log in, what they click on, how long pages are open. But they completely miss the quality of that time. They can't tell if someone's actually thinking or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all the offline work that might be where real learning happens.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you spend on a page. For me, engagement happens in short, intense bursts when I'm genuinely interested or when the pressure is on—not in consistent daily activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually understood the material deeply despite my sporadic attendance and incomplete assignments. It wouldn't capture my hands-on studying, my effective cramming sessions, or the fact that I was dealing with moderate anxiety and low resilience throughout. My high exam scores tell a different story than my engagement metrics suggest.
